enrichment.short_description: Microsoft Project is Microsoft's project and portfolio management application, offering Gantt-chart scheduling, resource management, and enterprise portfolio tools through tiered Microsoft 365-linked subscriptions.
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Project Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Project review 2026: pricing tiers from $10 to $55 per user, Gantt charts, resource management, and how it compares to Asana, Monday and Smartsheet.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Project (also marketed as Microsoft Planner and Project, or MS Project) is a project and portfolio management (PPM) software product first released by Microsoft in 1984, making it one of the oldest commercial project management tools still sold today. It is developed and owned by Microsoft Corporation, headquartered in Redmond, Washington, and is distributed as part of the Microsoft 365 family of subscription products. The tool grew out of Microsoft's early productivity software push in the 1980s and has evolved from a Windows desktop application into a hybrid suite that includes a cloud-based web app, a Power BI-integrated reporting layer, and an optional desktop client for advanced scheduling. Microsoft Project is built around three subscription tiers - Project Plan 1, Project Plan 3, and Project Plan 5 - which Microsoft has increasingly folded into its unified 'Planner' brand alongside Microsoft Planner (the lightweight Kanban-style task app). Plan 1 ($10/user/month) is a browser-based tool for grid, board, and timeline views aimed at small teams. Plan 3 ($30/user/month) adds the Project desktop client, resource management, timesheets, and more advanced scheduling. Plan 5 ($55/user/month) is aimed at enterprise Project Management Offices (PMOs) and adds portfolio selection and optimization, demand management, and enterprise resource planning. All plans integrate natively with the Microsoft 365 ecosystem, including Teams, SharePoint, Power BI, and Azure Active Directory / Entra ID for authentication. Core functionality centers on Gantt-chart based scheduling, critical path analysis, resource leveling, baseline tracking, and time/cost tracking against budgets. Enterprise tiers add portfolio-level dashboards, capacity planning, and what-if scenario analysis for prioritizing competing projects across an organization. Because it is built on the Microsoft Project file format (.mpp) that has been a de facto standard in enterprise PM for decades, it remains widely used by construction, engineering, IT, and government project teams that need detailed critical-path scheduling rather than the simpler Kanban/task-list approach of tools like Trello or Asana. Microsoft Project's target audience is mid-size to large organizations, especially those already standardized on Microsoft 365, that need formal, detailed project scheduling with resource and cost tracking, as opposed to lightweight startups. Its main competitors are Smartsheet, Oracle Primavera P6, Asana, monday.com, and Wrike; Primavera P6 is generally viewed as more powerful for very large construction and engineering megaprojects, while Asana and monday.com are viewed as more approachable for teams that do not need detailed critical-path scheduling. Microsoft Project's key differentiators are its deep Microsoft 365, Teams, and Power BI integration, its long history and enterprise trust, and its dual desktop-plus-cloud delivery model that lets power users keep using the traditional desktop scheduling engine while casual stakeholders view lightweight web and grid views.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Project is Microsoft's dedicated project and portfolio management application, first released in 1984 and now sold as a set of subscription plans (Project Plan 1, 3, and 5) alongside Microsoft Planner. It is built for detailed scheduling, budgeting, and resource management rather than lightweight task tracking, and integrates with the wider Microsoft 365 ecosystem including Teams, SharePoint, and Power BI.

The product spans a browser-based experience for grid, board, and timeline views, an optional Windows desktop client for advanced Gantt-chart scheduling and critical-path analysis, and enterprise portfolio management tools for organizations juggling many concurrent projects. It remains a standard tool in construction, engineering, IT, and government project management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of Microsoft Project is Gantt-chart scheduling with task dependencies, critical path calculation, and baseline tracking, which lets project managers see exactly how a schedule slip in one task cascades through a plan. Resource management tools allow leveling workloads across people and tracking cost against budget.

Higher tiers add timesheets, enterprise resource planning, and portfolio-level dashboards that help PMOs compare, prioritize, and optimize competing projects. Native Power BI connectors turn raw project data into executive-ready reporting, and Teams integration keeps project conversations and files attached to the plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Microsoft Project pricing is structured into three subscription tiers: Project Plan 1 at $10/user/month for web-based task and timeline tracking, Project Plan 3 at $30/user/month adding the desktop client and resource management, and Project Plan 5 at $55/user/month for full enterprise portfolio and demand management.

There is no permanent free tier, though Microsoft offers a 30-day free trial. Because pricing is per user per month, costs scale directly with team size, and organizations typically mix tiers, assigning Plan 5 only to PMO staff who need portfolio tools while giving individual contributors cheaper Plan 1 seats.

features.title: Power BI reporting
features.description: Native connectors turn project data into executive dashboards and reports.

features.title: Microsoft Teams integration
features.description: Keep project conversations, files, and updates attached to the plan.

features.title: Desktop client
features.description: A Windows desktop app for advanced offline scheduling, available on Plan 3 and 5.

pros: Deep integration with Microsoft 365 apps teams already use
Powerful, industry-standard Gantt and critical-path scheduling
Scales from small team task tracking to enterprise portfolio management
Familiar to generations of project managers trained on the .mpp format
Strong reporting via native Power BI connectors
cons: Steep learning curve for advanced scheduling features
Desktop client requires Windows; no native macOS desktop app
Pricing per user can get expensive for large teams needing Plan 3 or 5
Less intuitive than modern visual PM tools like Asana or monday.com for simple task tracking
Enterprise portfolio features require add-on licensing and setup complexity

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Project part of Microsoft 365?
faqs.answer: It is sold separately as add-on subscription plans rather than included in standard Microsoft 365 Business or Enterprise plans, though it shares the same billing and admin infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does Microsoft Project have a free version?
faqs.answer: There is no permanent free tier, but Microsoft offers a 30-day free trial on its paid plans.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Microsoft Project and Microsoft Planner?
faqs.answer: Planner is a lightweight task and Kanban tool, while Project is for formal scheduling with Gantt charts, dependencies, and resource leveling; Microsoft has merged both into a unified Planner app experience with Project plans layered on top.

faqs.question: Can Microsoft Project be used on Mac?
faqs.answer: The web-based Project for the web works in any browser, but the full desktop Project client is Windows only.

faqs.question: What file format does Microsoft Project use?
faqs.answer: .mpp, a proprietary format widely supported by import and export tools in competing project management software.

faqs.question: Who is Microsoft Project best suited for?
faqs.answer: Mid-size and enterprise teams with formal scheduling needs, especially organizations already standardized on Microsoft 365.

enrichment.short_description: Microsoft Teams is Microsoft's chat-based collaboration hub combining team messaging, video meetings, file sharing, and app integrations, available free or bundled with Microsoft 365.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid plans from $4/user/month for Teams Essentials)
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Teams Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Teams review 2026: free vs paid plans, Microsoft 365 bundling, video meeting limits, and how it stacks up against Slack, Zoom and Google Meet.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Teams is a chat-and-collaboration application developed by Microsoft, combining persistent team messaging, video and audio meetings, file storage and co-authoring (via SharePoint and OneDrive), and third-party app integrations into a single hub. It was announced in November 2016 and launched publicly in March 2017 as Microsoft's answer to Slack, replacing Skype for Business as Microsoft's primary business communication tool over the following years. Microsoft Teams is developed by Microsoft Corporation, headquartered in Redmond, Washington, and is one of the fastest-growing products in Microsoft's history, reporting over 320 million monthly active users in recent disclosures. Architecturally, Teams is built on Microsoft 365 infrastructure: each Team is backed by a Microsoft 365 Group, with a SharePoint document library for files, a shared OneNote notebook, and Exchange-based calendar and email integration. Channels within a team organize conversations by topic, and Teams supports threaded chat, @mentions, apps and tabs (including Planner, Power BI, and thousands of third-party integrations via the Teams app store), and Copilot AI features for meeting summaries and chat assistance. Teams is available as a standalone free product (5GB storage, unlimited chat, meetings up to 60 minutes with up to 100 participants) or as a paid product. Teams Essentials, at $4/user/month, is the only way to buy Teams without a full Microsoft 365 subscription and extends meetings to 30 hours with up to 300 participants and 10GB storage. Above that, Teams is bundled into Microsoft 365 Business Basic, Business Standard, Business Premium, and Enterprise E3/E5 plans, which add the full Office app suite, larger storage, and (at higher tiers) Teams Phone (PSTN calling), advanced security, and compliance features. Microsoft raised Microsoft 365 and Teams pricing in 2026 for new subscriptions and renewals, with enterprise tiers now ranging roughly $39 to $60 per user per month. Teams' primary audience is organizations already invested in the Microsoft ecosystem, from small businesses using Business Basic through large enterprises on E5 licenses, as well as frontline and education deployments (Teams for Frontline Workers, Teams for Education). Its main competitors are Slack (chat-first, seen as having a cleaner interface but weaker native video), Zoom (stronger dedicated video and webinar features), and Google Meet and Chat (bundled with Google Workspace). Teams' key differentiator is deep, native integration with Office apps, SharePoint, Exchange, and Azure Active Directory/Entra ID, making it the default choice for IT departments standardized on Microsoft 365 rather than a best-of-breed point solution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Teams is Microsoft's chat-and-meetings collaboration hub, launched in March 2017 as the company's response to Slack and the eventual replacement for Skype for Business. It combines persistent channel-based messaging, video and audio meetings, and file co-authoring into a single application tied to the Microsoft 365 ecosystem.

Each Team is backed by a Microsoft 365 Group with a SharePoint document library, shared OneNote notebook, and Exchange calendar integration, which is why Teams feels less like a standalone chat app and more like a front end for the rest of Office. Teams now reports well over 300 million monthly active users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Teams' core is channel-based team chat with threaded conversations, @mentions, and an app store offering thousands of tab and bot integrations, from Planner to third-party tools like Asana or Salesforce. Meetings support screen sharing, breakout rooms, together mode, and built-in whiteboarding.

Copilot AI features layered on top of Teams can summarize meetings, generate follow-up action items, and answer questions about chat history. Teams Phone, an add-on available at higher tiers, turns Teams into a cloud PBX capable of PSTN calling, replacing traditional desk phone systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Teams has a free tier with 5GB of storage and meetings capped at 60 minutes for up to 100 participants, which is enough for many small teams. Teams Essentials, at $4/user/month, is the cheapest paid tier and the only way to buy Teams without a full Microsoft 365 subscription, extending meetings to 30 hours and 300 participants.

Beyond Essentials, Teams is bundled into Microsoft 365 Business Basic, Standard, and Premium plans, and into Enterprise E3/E5 licenses, which add the full Office application suite, larger storage, and (at higher tiers) Teams Phone and advanced compliance features. Microsoft 365 and Teams pricing increased in 2026, with enterprise tiers now spanning roughly $39 to $60 per user per month.

features.description: Built-in visual collaboration tools for interactive meetings.

features.description: Collaborate with external partners without leaving your own tenant.

pros: Free tier is generous for small teams
Tight integration with Office apps and SharePoint
Handles both chat and enterprise-grade video meetings in one app
Strong compliance and security features for regulated industries at higher tiers
Scales from free personal use to large enterprise deployments
cons: Interface can feel cluttered compared to Slack
Full functionality requires a Microsoft 365 subscription
Free version limits meetings to 60 minutes
Can be resource-heavy on lower-end devices
Admin and tenant configuration can be complex for IT teams

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Teams free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free tier exists with unlimited chat and meetings up to 60 minutes for up to 100 participants.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest paid plan?
faqs.answer: Teams Essentials at $4/user/month.

faqs.question: Do I need Microsoft 365 to use Teams?
faqs.answer: No, Teams Essentials works standalone, but most advanced features come bundled with Microsoft 365 plans.

faqs.question: Does Teams replace Skype for Business?
faqs.answer: Yes, Microsoft retired Skype for Business in favor of Teams.

faqs.question: Can external guests join Teams meetings?
faqs.answer: Yes, guest access and shared channels support external collaboration.

faqs.question: What is Teams Phone?
faqs.answer: An add-on that turns Teams into a cloud PBX/VoIP phone system with PSTN calling.

enrichment.short_description: Microweber is an open-source, Laravel-based CMS and website builder that lets users edit pages live via drag-and-drop, with built-in ecommerce and blogging tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Microweber Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microweber review 2026: open-source drag-and-drop CMS pricing, ecommerce features, and how it compares to WordPress, Wix and Shopify.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microweber is an open-source content management system (CMS) and website builder that combines a live drag-and-drop visual editor with an underlying Laravel PHP framework. Development began around 2010 in Sofia, Bulgaria, by a small team of web developers, including Peter Ivanov, Boris Sokolov, Alex Raikov, and Borislav Iordanov, who ran a web-design agency and found existing CMS platforms too complex for everyday users and clients. The project's first stable 1.0.0 release shipped on February 16, 2015, built on the Laravel 5 framework, and the company behind it is headquartered in Sofia, Bulgaria. In 2018, with version 1.1.0, Microweber switched its license from GPL to MIT, making it more permissive for commercial and open-source reuse; the project has since been recognized as one of Bulgaria's notable startups. Unlike traditional CMSs that separate a back-end admin panel from a front-end preview, Microweber lets users edit content directly on the live page, dragging in modules (text, images, galleries, forms, shop products) and editing text inline, similar to a page builder like Elementor or Webflow but built into the CMS core rather than as a plugin. It ships with built-in ecommerce functionality (product catalogs, cart, checkout, payment gateway integration via Omnipay), blogging, a template and theme marketplace, and a REST API and module system for developers to extend functionality. Because it is Laravel-based and open source, it can be self-hosted for free on any PHP/MySQL-compatible server, and its source code is available on GitHub under the MIT license. Commercially, Microweber offers a dual model: the core CMS is free to download and self-host, while Microweber.com (and microweber.org) also sell managed cloud-hosted plans aimed at non-technical users and small businesses, bundling hosting, storage, and site/page limits into tiered monthly plans, plus an Enterprise and reseller license for hosting companies and agencies that want to white-label Microweber as a SaaS product. Its target audience spans hobbyists and freelancers who want a free self-hosted site builder, small businesses and agencies wanting an all-in-one hosted website-plus-shop solution, and developers who prefer a Laravel-based, code-extensible CMS over WordPress's PHP and plugin architecture. Its main competitors include WordPress (with Elementor and WooCommerce), Wix, Shopify, and Webflow; Microweber's key differentiator is being fully open source and self-hostable while still offering the live-editing, drag-and-drop experience typically associated with proprietary closed SaaS builders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microweber is an open-source CMS and website builder that grew out of a Sofia, Bulgaria web-design agency's frustration with existing CMS platforms being too complicated for everyday clients. Built on the Laravel PHP framework, its first stable release shipped in February 2015, and the project switched to the permissive MIT license in 2018.

What sets Microweber apart from a typical CMS is that editing happens directly on the live page rather than in a separate admin preview; users drag modules like text, images, and shop products straight onto the page and edit them inline, closer to a page-builder experience than a traditional back-end CMS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Microweber ships with a built-in ecommerce module (product catalog, cart, checkout, and Omnipay payment gateway integration), blogging tools, and a marketplace of templates and modules, so a single install can cover a marketing site, blog, and online shop without extra plugins.

Because the codebase is open source and Laravel-based, developers can extend it with custom modules and a REST API, and anyone can self-host it for free on a standard PHP/MySQL server. Microweber.com and microweber.org also sell managed cloud hosting for users who don't want to manage a server themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Microweber CMS is free and open source, and can be downloaded from GitHub and self-hosted at no cost beyond hosting infrastructure. For users who want a managed experience, Microweber.com sells tiered cloud plans that bundle hosting, storage, and website/page limits, scaling from single-site individual plans up to multi-site agency plans.

An Enterprise and reseller license is also available for hosting companies and agencies that want to offer Microweber as a white-labeled SaaS product under their own brand, positioning Microweber as both a free developer tool and a commercial hosted product depending on the customer's needs.

cons: Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem than WordPress

Smaller company and community support team than major competitors

Advanced customization requires developer/Laravel familiarity

faqs.question: Is Microweber really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core CMS is open source under the MIT license and free to self-host; Microweber.com also sells optional managed cloud hosting plans.

faqs.question: What is Microweber built on?
faqs.answer: The Laravel PHP framework.

faqs.question: Does Microweber support ecommerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has built-in shop, cart, and payment gateway features via Omnipay.

faqs.question: How is Microweber different from WordPress?
faqs.answer: Microweber uses live, on-page drag-and-drop editing built into the CMS core rather than a separate builder plugin, and is Laravel-based rather than WordPress's custom PHP architecture.

faqs.question: Can agencies white-label Microweber?
faqs.answer: Yes, an Enterprise and reseller license lets hosting companies offer Microweber as a branded SaaS product.

faqs.question: Where is Microweber based?
faqs.answer: Sofia, Bulgaria.

enrichment.short_description: middlewarehq is an open-source, self-hostable platform that calculates DORA metrics, deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, and mean time to restore, from an engineering team's Git and CI/CD data.
enrichment.seo_title: middlewarehq Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: middlewarehq review 2026: open-source DORA metrics platform pricing, GitHub integration, self-hosting, and alternatives like LinearB and Jellyfish.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: middlewarehq (published as the open-source project at github.com/middlewarehq/middleware, also referenced as the dora-metrics repository) is a self-hosted engineering-metrics platform that computes the four DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) metrics, deployment frequency, lead time for changes, mean time to restore, and change failure rate, directly from a team's version-control and CI/CD data. It is built and maintained by Middleware, a company founded by Laduram Vishnoi (previously founder of the customer-support platform Acquire) in late 2022, initially funded with a $500,000 personal investment before the company raised a $6.5 to $7 million seed round led by 8VC, with participation from Fin Capital, Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch, and other angels. Middleware went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. Middleware's commercial product is a broader AI-based cloud and full-stack observability platform, but middlewarehq is its dedicated open-source DORA metrics tool, released under the Apache 2.0 license and written primarily in TypeScript. It is designed to be deployed via Docker, either self-hosted on a company's own infrastructure or, in the commercial version, offered as a hosted SaaS product. The core technical approach is to pull data from Git hosting providers (GitHub being the primary supported source, with the project's stated goal of eventually supporting metrics from git data regardless of other integrations) and CI/CD pipelines, then compute and visualize the four DORA metrics on dashboards that let engineering leaders benchmark their team's software delivery performance against the industry-standard DORA performance tiers of Elite, High, Medium, and Low. middlewarehq's target audience is engineering leaders, VPs of Engineering, and platform and DevOps teams who want objective, automatically computed delivery performance metrics without manually assembling spreadsheets from Jira and Git logs, and who prefer, or require for security and compliance reasons, a self-hostable open-source tool over a fully managed third-party SaaS. Its main competitors include LinearB, Jellyfish, Swarmia, Faros AI, Sleuth, and GitClear, most of which are closed-source commercial SaaS products; Google's own DORA research popularized the four metrics that all of these tools compute. middlewarehq's key differentiator is being free, open-source, and self-hostable, which appeals to engineering organizations wary of sending source-control metadata to a third-party SaaS vendor, while its parent company also offers a commercial hosted and managed version and broader observability tooling for teams that want a fully managed solution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: middlewarehq is the open-source DORA metrics project maintained by Middleware, a San Francisco startup founded by Laduram Vishnoi in late 2022 and backed by Y Combinator (W23) and an 8VC-led seed round. The project computes the four DORA metrics, deployment frequency, lead time for changes, mean time to restore, and change failure rate, directly from a team's Git and CI/CD data.

Released under the Apache 2.0 license and written in TypeScript, middlewarehq (also published as the dora-metrics repository) is designed to be deployed via Docker, either self-hosted by an engineering organization or run through Middleware's managed cloud offering.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's central feature is automatic DORA metric calculation pulled from GitHub activity and CI/CD pipeline data, removing the need for engineering leaders to manually assemble delivery-performance spreadsheets. Dashboards benchmark a team's results against the standard DORA performance tiers of Elite, High, Medium, and Low.

Because it is open source, engineering teams with strict data-residency or security requirements can self-host the entire stack rather than sending source-control metadata to a third-party SaaS vendor, while still getting the same metric definitions used by commercial competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The middlewarehq open-source project itself is completely free to self-host under the Apache 2.0 license, with Docker-based deployment and community support through GitHub. There is no license fee for running it on a company's own infrastructure.

Middleware, the company behind the project, also offers a broader commercial AI-based observability platform and managed hosting option for teams that would rather not operate the open-source stack themselves; pricing for that managed offering is arranged directly with the company rather than published as fixed self-serve tiers.

Smaller community and ecosystem than established commercial competitors
Limited to DORA metrics rather than broader engineering analytics out of the box
Documentation and support are community-driven for the open-source edition

faqs.question: Is middlewarehq free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core project is open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Who makes middlewarehq?
faqs.answer: Middleware, a Y Combinator (W23) and 8VC-backed startup founded by Laduram Vishnoi.

faqs.question: What are DORA metrics?
faqs.answer: Four metrics from DevOps Research and Assessment: deployment frequency, lead time for changes, mean time to restore, and change failure rate.

faqs.question: How is middlewarehq deployed?
faqs.answer: Via Docker, either self-hosted or as a managed cloud offering from Middleware.

faqs.question: Which Git providers does middlewarehq support?
faqs.answer: GitHub is the primary supported source, with broader Git data support as a stated project goal.

faqs.question: Who is middlewarehq for?
faqs.answer: Engineering leaders and DevOps or platform teams wanting objective software delivery performance metrics.

enrichment.short_description: Midjourney is a self-funded AI research lab and image-generation service that turns text prompts into high-quality AI-generated images and video, accessed via Discord and its own web app.
enrichment.company_size: Small team (self-funded, reportedly well under a few hundred employees)
enrichment.seo_title: Midjourney Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Midjourney review 2026: V7/V8 features, pricing plans from $10 to $120 per month, Discord vs web app, and how it compares to DALL-E and Stable Diffusion.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Midjourney is an independent AI research lab and product that generates images, and increasingly video, from text prompts using proprietary diffusion-based generative models. It was founded in August 2021 in San Francisco by David Holz, a former NASA researcher who had previously co-founded and led the hand-tracking hardware company Leap Motion. Midjourney entered open beta on July 12, 2022, and became one of the breakout consumer AI products of the 2022-2023 generative AI boom alongside OpenAI's DALL-E and Stability AI's Stable Diffusion. Unusually for a frontier AI company, Midjourney has never raised institutional venture capital and remains entirely self-funded and profitable, reportedly reaching around $300 million in revenue in fiscal 2024 according to PitchBook estimates, while operating with a comparatively small team. Midjourney's product is delivered primarily through Discord, where users issue '/imagine' commands with text prompts in official Midjourney Discord servers or their own servers via the Midjourney bot, as well as through a standalone web application (midjourney.com) that provides a more traditional prompt box, image gallery, and editing interface. Core capabilities include text-to-image generation, image variation and upscaling, image-to-image and style-reference blending, in-painting and editing, and, with newer model versions, short AI video generation. The company has iterated through numbered model versions (V4 through V7), with V7 bringing improved prompt coherence and image quality, and V8 Alpha, introduced around March 2026, adding native 2K resolution output, sharper text rendering within images, and roughly 5x faster generation than prior versions. Midjourney is sold on a subscription basis with four consumer and prosumer tiers: Basic ($10/month), Standard ($30/month), Pro ($60/month), and Mega ($120/month), each granting a different allotment of 'Fast' GPU generation minutes plus unlimited slower 'Relax' mode generations on Standard tier and above; annual billing carries roughly a 20 percent discount versus monthly. There is no longer a free tier for new users. In August 2025, Midjourney announced a strategic technology partnership with Meta to license its aesthetic and image-generation technology for use in future Meta AI products, an unusual deal for a company that has otherwise avoided outside investment or big-tech ownership stakes. Midjourney's target audience spans digital artists, illustrators, concept artists, marketers, and hobbyists who want high aesthetic-quality AI imagery, as well as increasingly game studios, ad agencies, and product-design teams. Its main competitors are OpenAI's DALL-E and GPT image generation, Stability AI's Stable Diffusion (and Stable Diffusion-based tools like Leonardo.Ai), Adobe Firefly, and Google's Imagen and Gemini image tools. Midjourney's key differentiators are its distinctive, widely praised aesthetic output quality, its self-funded independence from big-tech platform constraints, and its community-driven Discord-native workflow, though the web app has narrowed the gap for users who prefer a conventional SaaS interface over Discord.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Midjourney is a self-funded AI research lab founded by David Holz, a former NASA researcher and Leap Motion co-founder, in San Francisco in August 2021. It entered open beta in July 2022 and quickly became one of the most recognized names in generative AI image creation, known for producing distinctly polished, artistic output compared to some competitors.

Unlike almost every other frontier AI company, Midjourney has never taken outside venture funding, remaining profitable and independent while reportedly generating around $300 million in revenue in fiscal 2024. In August 2025 it struck a technology-licensing partnership with Meta, an unusual move for a company that has otherwise stayed independent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Midjourney generates images from text prompts using proprietary diffusion models, accessible through '/imagine' commands in Discord or through its own web application at midjourney.com. Beyond initial generation, users can upscale, create variations, blend reference images and styles, and edit specific regions of an image.

Newer model versions have expanded capability further: V7 improved prompt coherence and image quality, while V8 Alpha, introduced around March 2026, added native 2K resolution output, sharper in-image text rendering, and roughly 5x faster generation, alongside growing support for short AI-generated video.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Midjourney runs on four subscription tiers with no ongoing free access for new users: Basic at $10/month, Standard at $30/month, Pro at $60/month, and Mega at $120/month. Each tier includes an allotment of 'Fast' GPU generation minutes, with unlimited slower 'Relax' mode generation included from Standard tier upward.

Annual billing knocks roughly 20 percent off the monthly price. Higher tiers also unlock features like stealth mode, which keeps generations private from the public community feed, and larger Fast-mode allowances for heavy commercial users such as agencies and studios.

cons: No permanent free tier for new users

faqs.question: Is Midjourney free?
faqs.answer: No, Midjourney requires a paid subscription starting at $10/month; there is no ongoing free tier for new accounts.

faqs.question: Do I need Discord to use Midjourney?
faqs.answer: No, Midjourney also offers a standalone web app at midjourney.com in addition to its original Discord bot.

faqs.question: Who owns Midjourney?
faqs.answer: Midjourney is an independent, self-funded company founded by David Holz; it has not taken outside venture capital.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Fast and Relax mode?
faqs.answer: Fast mode uses paid GPU minutes for quick generation, while Relax mode is slower but unlimited on Standard tier and above.

faqs.question: Can I use Midjourney images commercially?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid subscribers generally receive commercial usage rights, subject to Midjourney's terms of service.

faqs.question: What is Midjourney's newest model?
faqs.answer: V8 Alpha, introduced around March 2026, with native 2K resolution and faster generation.

enrichment.short_description: Mighty Networks is an all-in-one community platform that lets creators and entrepreneurs combine memberships, online courses, events, and branded apps into a single network for their audience.
enrichment.seo_title: Mighty Networks Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mighty Networks review 2026: pricing plans from $95 per month, community and course features, branded apps, and how it compares to Circle and Kajabi.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mighty Networks is a SaaS platform that lets creators, coaches, and brands build a combined community, online-course, and membership business under one roof, rather than stitching together separate tools for forums, courses, events, and payments. It is led by founder and CEO Gina Bianchini, who previously co-founded the social-network platform Ning with Marc Andreessen in 2004 and later launched the community app Mightybell in 2011, which evolved into Mighty Networks. The company is headquartered in Palo Alto, California, and has raised roughly $67.9 million in funding, including a $50 million Series B round in April 2021 led by Owl Ventures, with earlier backing from First Round Capital, Intel Capital, and Floodgate. As of recent reporting the company employs roughly 130 to 180 people. The platform's core architecture combines four pillars: a discussion and community feed organized by topics and spaces, structured online courses and content libraries, live and in-person event hosting, and paid membership and subscription commerce, all under a single login and, on higher tiers, a fully branded native iOS and Android app called Mighty Pro. Members join a creator's 'Network,' which can be free, paid, or a mix of free and gated paid spaces, and creators can layer in one-time course purchases, recurring memberships, and bundles. Mighty Networks reports that creators on its platform collectively earned around $500 million in a recent year, positioning it within the broader creator-economy infrastructure category alongside Kajabi, Circle, Podia, and Patreon. Pricing is tiered: the Launch plan starts at $95/month and carries a 2 percent transaction fee on sales made through the platform; the Scale plan is $215/month; and the Growth plan is $354/month, with each tier raising member and course limits while reducing or removing transaction fees, plus features like advanced automation, more branding customization, and additional admin roles. Annual billing offers a further discount. Above these self-serve tiers, Mighty Pro, the native branded mobile app tier distributed through the Apple App Store and Google Play, is sold as separate custom and enterprise pricing arranged directly with Mighty Networks' sales team, aimed at larger creators and organizations that want a fully white-labeled app experience. Mighty Networks' target audience is course creators, coaches, membership-site owners, and brand communities that want to own their audience relationship rather than depending on social media algorithms. Its main competitors are Circle, Kajabi, Podia, Skool, and Discord (for community specifically); Mighty Networks differentiates itself by combining community, courses, events, and native branded mobile apps in one integrated product, rather than requiring creators to bolt together a course platform and a separate community tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mighty Networks is an all-in-one community and course platform founded by Gina Bianchini, who previously co-founded the social-networking platform Ning with Marc Andreessen and later launched the community app Mightybell in 2011, which evolved into Mighty Networks. The company is headquartered in Palo Alto, California, and has raised roughly $67.9 million in funding, including a $50 million Series B in 2021.

Rather than requiring creators to combine a separate course platform, community forum, and event tool, Mighty Networks bundles all of them, plus payments and, on higher tiers, a fully branded native mobile app, into a single product under one login.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's community layer organizes members into topic-based spaces and discussion feeds, while its course layer supports structured content libraries and cohort-based learning. Events can be hosted live within the platform or listed for in-person gatherings, and creators can charge for memberships, one-time course purchases, or bundles.

Mighty Pro, available as a separate enterprise offering, produces a fully branded native iOS and Android app for a creator's network, distributed through the Apple App Store and Google Play under the creator's own name rather than Mighty Networks'.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mighty Networks' self-serve pricing has three tiers: Launch at $95/month (with a 2 percent transaction fee on platform sales), Scale at $215/month, and Growth at $354/month, with higher tiers raising member and course limits while reducing or eliminating transaction fees. Annual billing offers a further discount.

Mighty Pro, the branded native app tier, is priced separately as a custom enterprise engagement arranged directly with Mighty Networks' sales team, reflecting the added cost of app-store submission, review, and maintenance for a fully white-labeled mobile app.

Branded native mobile app option (Mighty Pro) unusual among competitors
Strong creator earnings track record and established company history
Flexible monetization including one-time, subscription, and bundled pricing
Founder has deep social-network industry background from Ning
cons: Entry pricing at $95/month is higher than some course-only competitors
Transaction fees apply on the lowest tier
Learning curve to configure spaces, courses, and automations
Mighty Pro branded apps require separate custom and enterprise pricing
Less focused on pure chat/community-only use cases than Discord or Circle

faqs.question: What does Mighty Networks cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at $95/month for the Launch tier, rising to $215/month for Scale and $354/month for Growth.

faqs.question: Does Mighty Networks take a cut of sales?
faqs.answer: The Launch plan charges a 2 percent transaction fee; fees are reduced or removed on higher tiers.

faqs.question: Who founded Mighty Networks?
faqs.answer: Gina Bianchini, who previously co-founded Ning and earlier launched Mightybell in 2011.

faqs.question: Can I get a branded mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, via Mighty Pro, sold as a separate custom-priced enterprise plan.

faqs.question: What can I sell on Mighty Networks?
faqs.answer: Courses, memberships, events, and bundled content, all within one network.

faqs.question: How much funding has Mighty Networks raised?
faqs.answer: Roughly $67.9 million total, including a $50 million Series B in 2021.

enrichment.short_description: Milanote is a visual notes and moodboard tool that lets creative teams organize ideas, images, links, and to-dos on flexible drag-and-drop boards for projects like content planning and design.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid plans from $9.99/month per person, billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Milanote Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Milanote review 2026: free plan limits, pricing from $9.99 per month, visual boards for creative projects, and how it compares to Miro and Notion.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Milanote is a visual collaboration and note-taking application designed for creative work, moodboards, content planning, brainstorming, and project organization, built around flexible drag-and-drop boards rather than linear documents or spreadsheets. The company was founded in 2016 in Australia by Ollie Campbell, with Brett Warren also associated as a founder in company records; Milanote has been reported as a bootstrapped, profitable business rather than a venture-funded startup, with estimated annual recurring revenue in the low single-digit millions as of recent third-party estimates. The product's core metaphor is the 'board': a free-form canvas onto which users drag notes, images, links, files, and to-do lists, then arrange and connect them visually, similar in spirit to a corkboard or a Pinterest-style moodboard, but combined with note-taking and light project-management features like task checklists. Boards can be nested inside other boards to build hierarchical project structures, and Milanote provides a library of templates for common creative workflows such as content calendars, mood boards, script and storyboard planning, and user research. It supports drag-and-drop file and image uploads, a browser extension and mobile apps for capturing inspiration on the go, real-time collaboration with comments, and export options for sharing boards outside the app. Milanote uses a freemium pricing model. The free plan functions as a permanent, not time-limited, trial capped at 100 total cards (notes, images, and links combined) and 10 file uploads, which is enough for evaluating the product or running very small personal projects. Paid plans are priced per person: roughly $9.99/month per person when billed annually, or around $12.50/month billed monthly, unlocks unlimited cards, unlimited file uploads, and additional storage. A separate 'Upgrade your team' plan (around $49/month base) and team plans for 10, 20, or 50 members offer bulk discounts of roughly 50 percent versus per-seat pricing. Milanote's target audience is designers, writers, marketers, agencies, and other creative professionals who need to organize inspiration and plan visual and content projects, as opposed to software teams needing kanban or sprint tools. Its main competitors are Miro and Mural (broader whiteboarding, more enterprise-oriented), Notion (document and database-centric rather than visual-canvas-centric), Trello (kanban rather than free-form canvas), and Pinterest (inspiration-gathering but not project planning). Milanote's key differentiator is its purpose-built focus on creative planning workflows, moodboards, storyboards, content calendars, with a simpler, lighter-weight interface than general-purpose whiteboard tools like Miro.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Milanote is a visual notes and moodboard application founded in Australia in 2016, built around free-form drag-and-drop boards rather than linear documents, spreadsheets, or kanban columns. The company has been widely reported as bootstrapped and profitable rather than venture-funded.

Its central idea is the board: a blank canvas where users place notes, images, links, files, and to-do items, then arrange and connect them visually, closer to a corkboard or Pinterest board than a traditional productivity app, but with light task-tracking built in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Boards in Milanote can be nested inside other boards to build hierarchical project structures, and a library of templates covers common creative workflows like moodboards, content calendars, and storyboards. A browser extension and mobile apps make it easy to capture inspiration, images, links, screenshots, directly into a board while browsing.

Real-time collaboration lets teams comment and edit boards together, and content can be exported or shared outside the app for presentations and client reviews. Because boards are visual rather than list-based, Milanote tends to suit early-stage ideation and planning better than execution-focused task tracking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Milanote's free plan has no time limit but caps usage at 100 total cards (notes, images, and links combined) and 10 file uploads, which works for small personal projects or evaluating the tool before upgrading. Paid individual plans start around $9.99/month per person when billed annually, or about $12.50/month billed monthly, and remove the card and upload caps.

Team pricing is available for groups of 10, 20, or 50 members at roughly a 50 percent discount versus per-seat individual pricing, along with a separate 'Upgrade your team' base plan starting around $49/month for smaller teams that don't fit the standard tiers.

Generous permanent free plan for evaluating the tool
Templates speed up common creative planning workflows
Nested boards support complex project hierarchies
Lightweight compared to heavier whiteboard tools like Miro
cons: Free plan's 100-card limit is restrictive for larger projects
Less suited to structured project or task management than dedicated PM tools
Team plans add up in cost for larger organizations
Fewer enterprise and security features than larger whiteboard competitors
Limited native integrations compared to Notion or Miro

faqs.question: Is Milanote free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a permanent free plan exists, limited to 100 cards and 10 file uploads.

faqs.question: How much does Milanote cost?
faqs.answer: Paid individual plans start around $9.99/month per person billed annually.

faqs.question: Who founded Milanote?
faqs.answer: Milanote was founded in 2016 in Australia.

faqs.question: Is Milanote good for project management?
faqs.answer: It is better suited to visual planning and brainstorming than formal task and project tracking.

faqs.question: Does Milanote support team collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, with real-time collaboration, comments, and team plans for 10, 20, or 50 members.

faqs.question: What is Milanote best used for?
faqs.answer: Moodboards, content planning, brainstorming, and organizing creative projects.

enrichment.short_description: Milvus is an open-source, distributed vector database built for storing and searching billions of high-dimensional embeddings, used for AI applications like semantic search, RAG, and recommendation systems.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source, self-hosted); Zilliz Cloud from a free tier or roughly $99/month for dedicated clusters
enrichment.seo_title: Milvus Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Milvus review 2026: open-source vector database features, Zilliz Cloud pricing, RAG and AI search use cases, and how it compares to Pinecone and Weaviate.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Milvus is an open-source, distributed vector database purpose-built for storing, indexing, and searching billions of high-dimensional embedding vectors, the numerical representations used by modern AI systems for semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), recommendation engines, and image and video similarity search. Milvus was originally developed starting in 2018 by Zilliz, a company founded in 2017 and headquartered in Redwood City, California, and was open-sourced in 2019. In 2020, Zilliz donated Milvus to the Linux Foundation's LF AI and Data Foundation, making it a vendor-neutral, community-governed open-source project rather than a purely Zilliz-controlled codebase, though Zilliz remains the primary corporate steward and largest contributor. Milvus is released under the Apache 2.0 license and has become the most widely adopted open-source vector database, with more than 42,000 GitHub stars. Technically, Milvus is designed as a cloud-native, horizontally scalable system that separates storage and compute, supports multiple approximate-nearest-neighbor (ANN) indexing algorithms (including HNSW, IVF, and DiskANN), and can run as a lightweight single-node deployment (Milvus Lite, embedded in a Python process), a standalone Docker deployment for small-to-medium workloads, or a fully distributed Kubernetes-based cluster (Milvus Cluster) for massive-scale production workloads spanning billions of vectors. It integrates with popular AI and ML frameworks and orchestration libraries such as LangChain, LlamaIndex, and Hugging Face, and supports hybrid search combining vector similarity with traditional scalar and metadata filtering. Zilliz funds Milvus's development and monetizes it primarily through Zilliz Cloud, a fully managed, hosted version of Milvus. Zilliz has raised roughly $113 million across three funding rounds, including a $43 million Series B in November 2020 led by Hillhouse Capital and a further $60 million round in August 2022 led by Prosperity7 Ventures, alongside earlier funding from Yunqi Partners and 5Y Capital. Zilliz Cloud pricing includes a free tier (roughly 5GB storage and 2.5 million vCUs per month), a pay-as-you-go Serverless tier (around $4 per million vCUs), and Dedicated cluster tiers starting around $99/month, with a 30-day free trial on paid tiers. The self-hosted, open-source version of Milvus remains completely free to run on a company's own infrastructure. Milvus's target audience is AI and ML engineering teams building semantic search, RAG pipelines for large language model applications, recommendation systems, and computer-vision similarity search, ranging from individual developers running Milvus Lite locally to large enterprises running distributed Milvus clusters or Zilliz Cloud. Its main competitors are Pinecone (fully managed, closed-source), Weaviate, Qdrant, Chroma, and pgvector (a PostgreSQL extension); Milvus differentiates itself through its open-source, vendor-neutral governance under the Linux Foundation, proven large-scale distributed architecture, and the option to move between self-hosted open source and Zilliz's managed cloud without changing databases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Milvus is an open-source, distributed vector database built to store and search billions of high-dimensional embedding vectors, the numerical representations that power modern AI applications like semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Zilliz, the company that started building Milvus in 2018, open-sourced it in 2019 and donated it to the Linux Foundation's LF AI and Data Foundation in 2020, making it a vendor-neutral open-source project.

Zilliz was founded in 2017 and is headquartered in Redwood City, California; it has raised roughly $113 million in funding, including a $43 million Series B in 2020 and a $60 million round in 2022, and continues to lead Milvus's open-source development while selling a managed cloud version called Zilliz Cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Milvus supports multiple approximate-nearest-neighbor indexing algorithms, including HNSW, IVF, and DiskANN, and can combine vector similarity search with traditional metadata filtering for hybrid search. It is cloud-native and horizontally scalable, separating storage from compute so it can grow to handle billions of vectors in production.

Deployment options range from Milvus Lite, an embedded version that runs inside a Python process for local development, to a standalone Docker deployment for small workloads, up to a fully distributed Kubernetes-based cluster for large-scale production use. It integrates directly with popular AI frameworks like LangChain, LlamaIndex, and Hugging Face.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The open-source, self-hosted version of Milvus is completely free under the Apache 2.0 license, with no usage limits beyond what a company's own infrastructure can support. This makes it a common choice for teams that want full control over their vector search infrastructure and data.

Zilliz Cloud, the managed hosting option, offers a free tier with roughly 5GB of storage and 2.5 million vCUs per month, a pay-as-you-go Serverless tier priced around $4 per million vCUs, and Dedicated cluster tiers starting around $99/month for teams that want guaranteed, isolated compute resources without operating Milvus themselves.

features.title: Distributed, scalable architecture
features.description: Cloud-native design that separates storage and compute for horizontal scaling.

features.title: Multiple ANN index types
features.description: Supports HNSW, IVF, DiskANN, and other approximate-nearest-neighbor algorithms.

features.title: Hybrid search
features.description: Combine vector similarity search with scalar and metadata filtering.

features.title: Milvus Lite
features.description: Lightweight embedded deployment for local development inside a Python process.

features.title: Kubernetes-based clustering
features.description: Distributed Milvus Cluster deployment for billion-scale production workloads.

features.title: AI framework integrations
features.description: Native integration with LangChain, LlamaIndex, and Hugging Face.

features.title: Zilliz Cloud managed hosting
features.description: Fully managed version of Milvus with free, serverless, and dedicated tiers.

features.title: Open governance
features.description: Apache 2.0 license under the Linux Foundation's LF AI and Data Foundation.

pros: Completely free and open source for self-hosted use
Proven to scale to billions of vectors in production
Vendor-neutral governance under the Linux Foundation
Flexible deployment options from embedded and local to distributed clusters
Strong ecosystem integration with RAG and LLM tooling
cons: Self-hosting a distributed cluster requires significant DevOps expertise
Zilliz Cloud costs can scale with usage for large workloads
Steeper learning curve than simpler vector stores like Chroma
Feature differences exist between Milvus Lite and full cluster deployments
Competing managed alternatives like Pinecone offer simpler zero-ops onboarding

faqs.question: Is Milvus free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source, self-hosted version is completely free under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: Who created Milvus?
faqs.answer: Zilliz, a company founded in 2017 and headquartered in Redwood City, California, started developing Milvus in 2018 and open-sourced it in 2019.

faqs.question: What is Zilliz Cloud?
faqs.answer: A fully managed, hosted version of Milvus offered by Zilliz, with free, serverless, and dedicated pricing tiers.

faqs.question: What is Milvus used for?
faqs.answer: Semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for LLMs, recommendation systems, and image or video similarity search.

faqs.question: How large can Milvus scale?
faqs.answer: Milvus's distributed cluster architecture is designed to handle billions of vectors in production.

faqs.question: Is Milvus governed by Zilliz alone?
faqs.answer: No, Milvus was donated to the Linux Foundation's LF AI and Data Foundation in 2020, making it a vendor-neutral open-source project.

use_cases.description: Store and retrieve embeddings to ground large language model responses in factual data.

use_cases.title: Semantic and multimodal search
use_cases.description: Search text, images, or other content by meaning rather than exact keyword match.

use_cases.title: Recommendation and similarity matching
use_cases.description: Find similar items or users based on vector embeddings.
